question: Define the terms 'entity set' and 'relationship set' in the context of an ER model. Provide a concrete example for each using a university database scenario.
expectedPoints: Definition of an entity set: A collection of similar entities (e.g., objects) that share the same attributes.
Definition of a relationship set: A set of relationships of the same type that associate entities from multiple entity sets.
Clear example of an entity set: The 'Student' entity set, with entities representing individual students like 'Alice' and 'Bob'.
Clear example of a relationship set: The 'Enrolls' relationship set, linking a 'Student' entity to a 'Course' entity.

question: Explain the concept of 'mapping cardinalities' in an ER diagram. Describe the four main types (one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, many-to-many) and provide a real-world example for each within a single, cohesive business context like a company.
expectedPoints: Definition of mapping cardinality: The number of entities from one entity set that can be associated with entities from another entity set via a relationship.
Description and example of one-to-one (e.g., a 'Company' entity is managed by exactly one 'CEO' entity, and a CEO manages one company).
Description and example of one-to-many (e.g., one 'Department' entity has many 'Employee' entities, but an employee belongs to one department).
Description and example of many-to-one (logically the same as one-to-many, just described from the opposite perspective).
Description and example of many-to-many (e.g., an 'Employee' entity can work on many 'Project' entities, and a project has many employees assigned).

question: A library database must track members, books, and loans. Analyze the structural constraints for the 'Borrows' relationship between the 'Member' and 'Book' entity sets. Consider both cardinality ratios and participation constraints. Develop a scenario where a member can borrow multiple books, but a specific book copy can only be borrowed by one member at a time. Furthermore, discuss if participation should be total or partial for each entity and justify your reasoning.
expectedPoints: Identification of cardinality ratio: The relationship is Many-to-One from Book to Member (a member can borrow many books, but a specific book copy is borrowed by at most one member at a time).
Analysis of participation for 'Book': Partial participation. Not every book is necessarily on loan at all times; some remain on the shelf.
Analysis of participation for 'Member': Partial participation. A member is still a member even if they have not currently borrowed any books.
Justification for each participation constraint based on the real-world operational rules of a library.
Discussion of how these constraints would be implemented in the schema (e.g., using foreign keys and NULL constraints).
